chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content:
Compiler Design. (3-0). Credit 3. Advanced topics in compiler writing; parser generators and compiler-compilers; dynamic storage and scope resolution; data flow analysis and code optimization. Prerequisite: CSCE 434.

Project Logistics
The language used for the project is Java. No other language is allowed.
For the first 2 project assignments, students must work individually.
From the third project assignment till the last (6th), students must work in pairs, from the same section.
Students may choose to work individually, but should be aware that the project’s requirement is not reduced proportionally.
Students may break from the pair only after the third project assignment, but must find themselves a new partner for the last three project assignments.
After the fourth assignment, if the pair structure still does not work for a student, that student goes solo.
Public testcases requirement:
Each student must contribute at least 1 test to the set of public testcases for each project assignment to a shared GitHub repository. Each test is required to satisfy 3 requirements:
1. Unique: the test must be reasonably different from existing tests.
2. On time: the test must be committed and pushed to the shared repository 72 hours before the assignment deadline. The cut-off for test submission is 24 hours before the assignment deadline.
3. Usable: the test must have no inconsistency. It is the author’s responsibility to fix issues by the cut-off.
5% of full credit penalty each if the test is duplicated, late, or inconsistent; the penalty can be applied at any time after the deadline.
No test submission penalty: 17% of full credit
Project assignments grading:
Each front-end project assignment (PA 1-3) grade:
20%: passing 80% of public tests
80%: passing 100% of private tests
Back-end project assignments (PA 4-6) will be graded as a whole at the end of the semester. Further details of the grading are in the project description.

Campuswire will be the main communication and Q&A platform, not Canvas. The class link and code to join will be provided in the first class meeting. If you drop the class, please leave Campuswire. Interfering with or disrupting Campuswire will be reported and result in disciplinary actions according to Student Rules.
Unless regarding personal matters (e.g., grade), questions are to be posted on Campuswire first, for the benefit of other students. Students who wish to discuss personal matters should see the course staff during office hours.
Students are encouraged to participate in the forum and help answer questions. Campuswire is mainly made for students by students.
Students must never post codes or solutions on Campuswire for any reason. If asking for implementation clarification/guide, describe what has been done in pseudocode. Posts with codes/solutions will be deleted, and the poster might be banned from Campuswire.
All posts to Campuswire must be public (visible to everyone) and un-anonymous. Private posts or messages to the course staff will be deleted.
The order of getting answers is: Campuswire or in class, or office hours.
Emailing should be last.
Students must not treat Campuswire as a de-facto email system (e.g., make a post visible to TA and instructor only and discuss code/solution of the student). Those who violate will be banned from Campuswire.
The course staff will not check Campuswire during non-working hours, e.g., weekends and late hours.
In the past, students tended to wait until the due date and then panic and ask questions. This is ineffective and discouraged. To enforce better behavior, the course staff will not check Campuswire during the last 6 hours before the deadline.

Aside from Campuswire posts, the instructor will make class announcements via a mailing list: csce-compiler-fall2025@lists.tamu.edu - not Canvas. Students should make sure they can receive emails from the mailing list. All emails to the mailing list are assumed to be read after 12 hours. Allow 24 hours after registering for the class for the roster to be synchronized with the mailing list. Past emails may be found on the Google Group page.
How to succeed in this course (or any course):
Here are a few tips:
Attend class meetings regularly.
Take notes during lectures.
Read assigned readings before the lecture and your notes after class. Read the assigned readings again after class.
Ask questions and participate in discussions in class and on Campuswire.
Campuswire is FYBY - For You and By You.
Practice, practice, practice. For each credit hour, you are expected to spend 3 hours per week outside of class. Allow sufficient time to absorb the materials.
Study for exams. Group study/review for exams is allowed. Students in the past have found group review sessions and crowd-sourcing practice tests and review sheets helpful.
Complete assignments on time.
Visit the course staff during office hours to get questions answered.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content:
CSCE 434.
This course is a continuation of a Programming Languages course; hence, previous knowledge is expected. Sufficient programming experience and background are required.
Technical Skills Expectations:
Students should be familiar with installing libraries/packages, configuring environments, executing the compilers, and running compiled programs (with or without an IDE). Familiarity with the Linux environment is needed. Experience with Git is required.
Prerequisites Waiver:
Graduate students who are unsure about prerequisites should discuss with the instructor.
Course Auditing:
Auditing a course must go through a formal approval process per student rules. This is often for very rare extenuating scenarios, i.e., the reason cannot be to prepare to take the class next semester, or to effectively retake a course without being enrolled.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Understand the translation process from user code to machine code.
Describe major components of a typical modern compiler and their function.
Apply parsing techniques such as LL(1), SLR(1), and LR(1).
Design and implement sound dataflow analyses.
Understand and implement code optimization techniques.
Develop a front end and a back end of an optimizing compiler that can generate executable machine code from source code.

chunks.title: Textbook and
chunks.content:
[Dragon] Compilers: Principles, Techniques, & Tools (aka the Dragon book)
Authors: Alfred Aho, Monica Lam, Ravi Sethi, Jeffrey Ullman
Publication Date: 2006
Edition: 2nd
[EaC] Engineering: A Compiler
Authors: Keith Cooper, Linda Torczon
Publication Date: 2011
Edition: 2nd
URL for Resource: https://go.oreilly.com/TAMU/library/view/-/9780080916613
Notes:
The 2nd edition is no longer in print. The new, 3rd edition (ISBN 9780128154120) is available in the bookstore. Using either edition will be fine for readings. Assigned readings are based on 2nd edition.
[PPA] Principles of Program Analysis
Authors: Fleming Nielson, Hanne Riis Nielson, Chris Hankin
Publication Date: 1999
Edition: 2nd
URL for Resource: https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-3-662-03811-6
Notes:
We will use Chapters 1 and 2
Google Drive
Notes:
Students must use TAMU credentials to access the shared drive. Allow 24 hours after registering for the class to access this shared drive.

Some assessments will be conducted online using Respondus Lockdown Browser with Monitor - and will be referred to as quizzes. There is no cost to students to use the software. Students are required to have a webcam, which may be built into the computer or may be an external camera. The software is available for Mac, PC, and Chromebooks. It is important to test LockDown Browser and the webcam before using the application in a quiz. There will be an opportunity for students to practice and set up the environment. Students must ensure that they have a functional environment during the course to take quizzes. Unless due to extended excused absences, no makeup is possible.
Note: the “Available Until” date and time is a hard limit, triggering a submission automatically, and overriding any remaining time on the timer. Each quiz is not longer than 1 hour.
University Library (Evans Annex) Tech Bar: Any Texas A&M student with an active NetID can check out electronics such as Chromebooks, chargers, and webcams from the Tech Bar for free. Equipment reservations are available up to seven days in advance. Walkups are also possible. For Chromebooks, use https://tamu.libcal.com/reserve/chromebooksAnnex ; for accessories, use https://tamu.libcal.com/reserve/computeraccessoriesAnnex
Respondus should be available for use on these Chromebooks as a Chrome extension.
Alternatively, students may be able to take Respondus Lockdown Browser exams in select computer labs on campus. See TAMU—Open Access Lab (OAL) Computer Workstation Locations ( http://tx.ag/TAMUoal ) for more information.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
The course grade is based on the following components:
Quizzes: 4 online quizzes: 15% of grade. Online quizzes are conducted online using Respondus Lockdown Browser. All quizzes are open-note only. Each will carry a different weight.
Exams: 1 midterm exam - 20% of grade, and 1 non-cumulative final exam - 25% of grade). Exams areopen book, open note, closed internet, and closed neighbor. Exams are conducted in-person.
Project: 40% of grade. This will be a semester-long project building a complete optimizing compiler with multiple parts, with later components depending on previous ones.
Graduate students are subject to additional requirements as follows:
Extra and/or different questions on quizzes, midterm, and final exams.
Additional features/optimizations required in project assignments.
Passing additional test cases when turning in project assignments to earn full credit.

Course Grading Scale: F < 48% ≤ D < 58% ≤ C < 72% ≤ B < 86% ≤ A

Cutpoints are non-negotiable. Furthermore, people are cutpoints; whole-numbered percentages are not. In other words, the cutpoint for a particular grade is the lowest-scoring person who got assigned that grade.
The grade assigned is not on a curve. In this course, no curving will happen.
There will be no extra-credit assignments.
The grades may be audited randomly at any point in the course. This may result in increased/decreased scores for released grades. Affected students will be notified.

Regrading can be requested for any graded materials. The guidelines are:
The request must be made on Gradescope and within a specified window. Outside of this window, no grading requests will be accepted. No requests via email will be accepted. No exceptions will be made.
The request must include a reasonable justification and/or documentation.
Non-code submissions (e.g., writing assignments, exams): If the handwriting is not legible, what the graders guess is final, and the regrade request will be denied.
The following kinds of submissions will get 0 without grading, and regrading will be denied:
Disorganized, messy submissions.
Wrong submissions or submissions that did not follow the instructions (e.g., submitting different file types, not matching the assignment outline with the Gradescope submission, using banned libraries).
Uncompilable programming assignments on Gradescope. “It works on my machine” argument will be ignored.

Per Student Rule 10, the instructor shall give an incomplete grade only in cases due to an authorized absence or other causes beyond the control of the student, such as illness or personal emergency. No exceptions will be made. Students are required to furnish appropriate documentation. Under no circumstances can the “I” grade be given to a student who wishes to:
Do additional work after the course deadline to improve a grade
Redo the coursework in a subsequent semester to improve the current grade
Awarding an incomplete is always at the discretion of the instructor.

The grade for individual assignments and the course is assigned to each student based on concrete, quantifiable, objectively evaluated submissions and exams.
Grade is not given to students. Hence, in the interest of fairness and consistency, requests for grade increases that are inconsistent with the stated grading scale are denied.
Here is an incomplete list of hypothetical requests:
(I feel) I do not deserve [the grade/score] because of [reason(s)]
(I feel) The (grading/course) rubric is unbalanced due to [reason(s)]
I spend [huge number]% of my time on X which accounts for only [small number]% of the grade, [elaborate on how the grading should be here]
I do very well on other assignments, but I panicked during tests
With the grade lower than . . . , I will be [insert a consequence here]
I worked very hard for the class, but because of [reason(s)] I failed
I have too many other responsibilities.
I am really close to getting a . . .
I am willing to do extra work
I have a good GPA so far
I have never failed a class before.
I have never taken a course in this area before
I have never gotten a grade as low as . . . before
It doesn’t hurt to ask
I am about to graduate.
This course is too hard

Submission of Assignments and Grade Posting:
All assignments must be turned in electronically on Gradescope. Email submissions will not be accepted. Emails with submission attachments will be deleted without response. No exceptions will be made.
Students are advised not to use Gradescope as a debugging tool, as the autograder might be buggy. Students, therefore, should focus on debugging locally.
Grade posting: The score shown in Gradescope will be imported into Canvas as-is after the deadline (and regrading).

Assignment Deadlines and Late Penalty:
All assignments have established deadlines. Submitting a deliverable after the deadline is late work. Late submission is allowed but will incur a late penalty. No late work outside the grace period is accepted unless due to university-approved absences. The course staff will not answer assignment questions during the grace period.
Should deadline discrepancies arise between the syllabus, assignment description PDF, and Gradescope: the deadline on Gradescope takes precedence and is the official deadline.
Project assignments: Unless a project assignment is restricted as "hard-deadline", late submission is allowed with a 39-hour grace period. No late work outside the grace period is accepted unless due to university-approved absences. The course staff will not answer assignment questions during the grace period.
Late submission will be graded as if it were on time, and then a late penalty will be deducted (out of full credit). Late penalty will be calculated as follows:
Let h be the whole number of hours late (rounding up), the penalty is
h²/24 + h. That is, a 12-hour-late assignment will receive -18% (out of 100% — full credit) as a penalty; 1 day late, -48%. At 36 hours late, the most one can score in an assignment is 10. At 39 hours late, the assignment receives no credit.
See the graph below:
Late penalty is waived in cases of university-approved absences
Excused absences: Students are required to furnish documentation for an excused absence by default. When possible, notify the course staff of excused absences before the deadline - not after. Refer to Student Rule 7 for information about excused absences. This course will strictly enforce Rule 7.
Computer crashes, malfunctions, and catastrophic loss of files are not valid excuses for not submitting work on time (and thus request for the waiver of the late penalty). Students are advised to turn in assignments early to avoid any issues due to emergencies.
Students are responsible for making sure submissions are successfully submitted on Gradescope. Unless due to global, public outages of Gradescope, networking connectivity problems are not excuses for missing or late Gradescope submissions.
Technology issues are not an excuse for missing a deadline. Make sure to prepare the environments for assignments well in advance of deadlines, and reach out to course staff early for help.
Students are responsible for ensuring submissions are successfully submitted on Gradescope.
The instructor reserves the right to define makeup work for an excused absence, which is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy.

chunks.title: Class Participation
chunks.content:
This course strictly enforces the University’s Attendance Policy, which states
The University views class attendance and participation as an individual student responsibility.
Students are expected to attend class and to complete all assignments.
No credit will be awarded for class attendance. If students miss class, it is their responsibility to catch up with the materials and announcements. The course staff will not repeat announcements that were made in class.
Attendance Certification:
As required by Federal aid regulations, the University requires an attendance certification of each student having begun engagement in an academically related activity. This course uses submissions of assignments, quizzes, homeworks as evidence to satisfy the required documentation. The attendance certification is due approximately at the end of the 3rd week. Students are hereby notified that by the due date - specifically 09/12/2025 5:00 PM , if students have zero submissions, they will not be certified of this course’s attendance. There will be no warning or communication should a student be at risk of not being certified. Failure of this certification might negatively impact students’ financial aid.

Unless due to excused absences, no makeup exam will be given. If an excused absence is expected, students should contact the instructor as soon as possible (with documentation). Makeup exams will be different from the original exams.
If a student misses an exam due to an excused absence, the student must take the makeup exam on the designated date and time:
Midterm: Monday, 10/6/25 at 7:00 PM in PETR 226
Final: Monday, 12/15/25 at 7:00 PM in PETR 226
Discuss with the instructor directly if rescheduling is required.

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Topics

Dragon = Aho, Lam, Sethi, Ullman

chunks.title: Course Evaluation
chunks.content:
Evaluations are a way for students to provide feedback regarding their instructor and the course after the delivery, including student perceptions of learning and the student experience in the course. Detailed feedback will enable the instructor to continuously tailor and improve teaching methods and course content to meet the course’s learning goals and the students’ academic needs. They are a requirement of the course and are key to continuing to provide students with the highest quality of teaching.
Texas A&M University utilizes HelioCampus-Assessment, formerly AEFIS, to collect student course evaluations. The evaluations are anonymous; the instructor and administration do not track who entered what responses. At no point in the process that the instructor knows the students’ identity or whether a student has completed the evaluation. Students are strongly encouraged to participate in the course evaluation. Refer to Office of Institutional Effectiveness & Evaluation page for more information. The instructor welcomes feedback during the course.
The Faculty Senate passed a Resolution in 2010 (FS.27.122) that specifically stated "The Faculty Senate opposes granting academic credit of any kind to students for completing course evaluations ...", this would include incentives such as bonus points. As such, there will be no credit for completing the course evaluation.

The instructor aims to create a learning experience that is as accessible and inclusive as possible. If a student anticipates or experiences any barriers to learning in this course, the instructor will consider creative solutions as long as they do not compromise the intent of the assessment or learning activities.
Students are strongly encouraged to contact Disability Resources and/or use the Disability Accommodation Portal ( https://disability.tamu.edu/about/aim/ ). It is better if the students get accommodations and do not need them, rather than trying without accommodations and finding later that such accommodations are needed.

Students are expected to treat the instructor and all other participants in the course with courtesy and respect — refer to Student Rule 21. Comments to others should be factual, constructive, and free from harassment. Unprofessional or disrespectful conduct will not be tolerated. Warnings will not be given. Students will need to contribute in intelligent, positive, and constructive ways within the course. Behaviors that are abusive, disruptive, or harassing may result in disciplinary actions as specified within the Student Rules.

Students may not record audio or video of any course activity unless the student has an approved accommodation from Disability Services permitting the recording. This accommodation letter must be presented to the instructor in advance of any recording being done. Students with permission to record classes are not permitted to redistribute audio or video recordings of statements or comments from the course to other individuals without the express permission of the faculty member and of any students who are recorded.

Materials used in this course are subject to copyright. Students are not allowed to reproduce, distribute, or post any lecture materials, assignments, exams, solutions, etc., publicly. Students may take notes and make copies of course materials for personal use. Students may not post course materials on commercial websites such as Course Hero and Chegg. Doing so is a copyright violation and, in some cases, may also be an academic integrity violation that will be dealt with accordingly.

The university views class attendance and participation as an individual student responsibility. Students are expected to attend class and to complete all assignments.
Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about excused absences, including definitions, and related documentation and timelines.

Students will be excused from attending class on the day of a graded activity or when attendance contributes to a student’s grade, for the reasons stated in Student Rule 7, or other reason deemed appropriate by the instructor.
Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about makeup work, including definitions, and related documentation and timelines.
Absences related to Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972 may necessitate a period of more than 30 days for make-up work, and the timeframe for make-up work should be agreed upon by the student and instructor” ( Student Rule 7, Section 7.4.1 ).
“The instructor is under no obligation to provide an opportunity for the student to make up work missed because of an unexcused absence” ( Student Rule 7, Section 7.4.2 ).
Students who request an excused absence are expected to uphold the Aggie Honor Code and Student Conduct Code. ( See Student Rule 24 .)

Plagiarism is one of the worst academic sins, for the plagiarist destroys trust among colleagues and the purpose of learning. Plagiarism is commonly defined as passing off as one’s own ideas, words, writings, code, etc., which belong to another person. By this definition, in this course, a student commits plagiarism if copying the work elsewhere, even with the permission of the author, and turns it in as one’s own.
The instructor encourages collaboration and the exchange of ideas among students. Collaboration is important for facilitating learning, and peers often can be a great resource. However, all assignments in this course are individual efforts. All assignments must be fully completed by only the student to whom they are assigned, without unauthorized aid. Any academic dishonesty will not be tolerated and will be severely dealt with. Consequences include a zero grade for the activity/assignment, course failure, and dismissal from the university, among others. Refer to the Academic Integrity Statement and Policy below. All involved parties will be penalized equally.
Specifically, students may discuss the assignments at a high level (e.g., concepts, approaches) with friends but absolutely no actual solution sharing (voluntarily or involuntarily) is allowed.
Code: Students are not allowed to use code found elsewhere (e.g., Google searches, online repositories, past offerings of the course). The use of a private code repository, with a detailed commit history, is recommended and may be requested at any time to answer authorship inquiries. Students also must not attempt to circumvent the objective of an assignment (e.g., using a banned library or a function to bypass steps outlined in the assignment)
Non-code: The write-up of homeworks must be done individually and must not be shared in any way or form.
No full or partial deliverables are allowed to be knowingly or unknowingly released in the public domain even after the deadlines and after the course is over. Students are solely responsible for securing all deliverables away from the public.
Group review sessions for exams are allowed and encouraged.
Posting assignments to or copying solutions from unauthorized sources is academic misconduct.
There have been past incidents where students used code from Chegg or similar tutoring websites; the use of such resources is not allowed.
Complicity
Every student should understand that complicity – helping or attempting to help another student commit an act of academic dishonesty – also constitutes academic dishonesty and carries the same punishment as cheating and plagiarism.
In other words, if providing a solution to another student, even if that student does not turn it in for credit, the provider has committed an act of academic dishonesty.
Occasionally, we find evidence of plagiarism after the assignments have been graded. For example, a prior assignment may be flagged when a new detector becomes available. For these reasons, you should treat your assignment grades as provisional. Any prior submission that is later found to constitute a violation will be reported to the Aggie Honor System Office (AHSO).

Upon accepting admission to Texas A&M University, a student immediately assumes a commitment to uphold the Honor Code, to accept responsibility for learning, and to follow the philosophy and rules of the Honor System. Ignorance of the rules does not exclude any member of the Texas A&M University community from the requirements or the processes of the Honor System. By submitting anything to the course, electronically or otherwise, students acknowledge and assert the Honor
Code, which states:
On my honor, as an Aggie, I have neither given nor received unauthorized aid on this academic work.
Often Honor Code violation is the result of panic when falling behind. Playing catch-up is never a good strategy when taking any courses, including this one. When in doubt about what constitutes a violation, students should contact the instructor. Do not engage in the “it is better to ask for forgiveness than permission” mentality.
Reporting
It is the responsibility of all members, including but not limited to students, staff, faculty, and teaching assistants of the Texas A&M University Community, to actively promote academic integrity. Instructors are expected to adhere to the policy pertaining to the reporting and adjudication of violations of the Aggie Code of Honor. Initiating formal procedures is a necessary and obligatory component of challenging and making known acts that violate the Aggie Code of Honor.
Fabrication
Any information (e.g., a doctor’s note for an excused absence, sections in a project report) that is found to have been fabricated, falsified, or misused will be submitted to the Aggie Honor System Office (AHSO) with severe penalties including but not limited to an F* for the course, or University expulsion, which the instructor has used before.
A Reminder of Rule 20.1.3
“Texas A&M University students are responsible for authenticating all work submitted to an instructor. If asked, students must be able to produce proof that the item submitted is indeed the work of that student. Students must keep appropriate records at all times. The inability to authenticate one’s work, should the instructor request it, may be sufficient grounds to initiate an academic misconduct case.”

chunks.title: Use of Generative AI
chunks.content:
The use of AI to gain a greater understanding of a topic is a great use of its capabilities. This is allowed.
Code: Students may use AI tools and submit AI-generated code, or the derivation from such code for the project assignments, as long as this use is properly documented in the code/comments and the final project report.
Writing: All non-code submissions (e.g., reports) must be generated by students themselves. Students must not have another entity write, produce, or generate any portion of a submission for them.
